text:
GASTROINTESTINAL MEDICAL ONCOLOGY — FOLLOW-UP CLINIC NOTE

Patient: Marcus T. Delgado
Date of Birth: 1965
Attending: Gastrointestinal Medical Oncology
Encounter Type: Follow-up / Disease Re-staging / Treatment Planning

---

CHIEF COMPLAINT

Mr. Delgado is a 59-year-old Hispanic male with known Stage IV sigmoid colon adenocarcinoma (KRAS G12D, MSS, TMB-low) presenting today for disease reassessment following recent restaging CT imaging, which unfortunately demonstrates progression of disease on second-line FOLFIRI plus ramucirumab. He is accompanied by his wife. We are meeting today to review the imaging findings in detail, discuss his current symptomatic status, and formulate a plan for third-line systemic therapy.

---

HISTORY OF PRESENT ILLNESS

Mr. Delgado was initially diagnosed with Stage IV sigmoid colon adenocarcinoma approximately 17 months ago, presenting with synchronous bilobar liver metastases (4 lesions, largest 3.8 cm in segment VI) at the time of diagnosis. His molecular profile was notable for a KRAS p.G12D mutation (VAF 42%), rendering him ineligible for anti-EGFR therapy. He also harbors co-mutations in APC, TP53, SMAD4, PIK3CA, and FBXW7 — all consistent with a left-sided colorectal adenocarcinoma with features of chromosomal instability.

He completed 12 cycles of first-line FOLFOX plus bevacizumab with a meaningful partial response — his CEA fell from 487 to 87 ng/mL and liver metastases decreased in size across all four lesions. Unfortunately, oxaliplatin was discontinued after cycle 10 secondary to grade 3 cumulative peripheral sensory neuropathy (bilateral hands and feet, involving the mid-calves and wrists), and he transitioned to maintenance 5-FU/leucovorin/bevacizumab after completing the planned 12 cycles.

At approximately 10 months from diagnosis, while on maintenance therapy, restaging CT demonstrated new bilateral pulmonary nodules (right lower lobe 1.1 cm, right upper lobe 0.7 cm), confirmed to represent metastatic colorectal adenocarcinoma on CT-guided lung biopsy (CK20+, CDX2+, CK7-, TTF-1-, KRAS G12D concordant). This represented his first disease progression event. After multidisciplinary tumor board discussion, he was transitioned to second-line FOLFIRI plus ramucirumab per the RAISE trial regimen.

He completed approximately 7 months on FOLFIRI/ramucirumab. After 4 cycles, restaging imaging showed disease stabilization with a modest decrease in the sum of liver metastases, and lung nodules were stable. However, his most recent restaging CT scan (reviewed today) now demonstrates clear radiographic progression: three additional new pulmonary nodules have appeared in the left lower lobe (0.8 cm), right middle lobe (0.6 cm), and lingula (0.5 cm); the segment VI liver metastasis has grown from 2.3 to 2.9 cm; and there is a new 0.8 cm lesion in hepatic segment V. His CEA has risen markedly to 287 ng/mL (from 112 ng/mL at last assessment) and CA 19-9 has climbed to 201 U/mL. This is consistent with progressive disease through his second-line regimen.

From a symptomatic standpoint, he reports increasing fatigue over the past 4-6 weeks. He describes it as a "heavy tiredness" that limits his daily activities — he can no longer work full days and has cut back significantly on his classroom teaching responsibilities. He also reports mild worsening abdominal discomfort, primarily epigastric and right upper quadrant in nature, which he attributes to his liver metastases. Appetite remains reasonable though he notes early satiety at meals. He denies nausea, vomiting, or change in bowel habits. He denies chest pain, hemoptysis, or worsening dyspnea. He denies headache, new neurological symptoms, or bone pain.

His oxaliplatin-induced peripheral neuropathy has improved to grade 1 — he now has intermittent mild tingling in his feet only, most noticeable after prolonged walking. Gabapentin has been tapered down. His hypertension has been a persistent management challenge through the course of bevacizumab and ramucirumab; his current antihypertensive regimen includes amlodipine 10 mg daily, lisinopril 20 mg daily, and hydrochlorothiazide 12.5 mg daily, with generally adequate blood pressure control.

He and his wife have been engaged and thoughtful partners in shared decision-making throughout his treatment course. He expresses a clear desire to continue active treatment if options remain available and if the treatment is tolerable, stating that he values both quantity and quality of time with his family. He understands that his disease has progressed through two lines of systemic therapy and that the likelihood of achieving a major response diminishes with each successive line, but wishes to pursue further treatment.

---

REVIEW OF SYSTEMS

Positive: Fatigue (moderate, worsening over 4-6 weeks), right upper quadrant and epigastric discomfort (mild to moderate, intermittent), early satiety, mild weight loss (approximately 4 lbs over the past 6 weeks), peripheral neuropathy in bilateral feet (residual, mild/grade 1). Negative: No fever, chills, or night sweats. No nausea or vomiting. No significant change in bowel habits (no diarrhea at present since irinotecan dose was reduced). No hematochezia or melena. No dysphagia or odynophagia. No chest pain or palpitations. No cough, hemoptysis, or significant dyspnea. No new headache, cognitive changes, seizure, or focal neurological deficits. No bone pain or pathological fracture. No lower extremity edema. No rash or skin changes.

---

PAST MEDICAL HISTORY

1. Stage IV sigmoid colon adenocarcinoma (KRAS G12D, MSS, TMB-low) — diagnosed approximately 17 months ago; disease now with hepatic, pulmonary involvement
2. Type 2 diabetes mellitus — well-controlled on metformin; HbA1c most recently in the low 7% range
3. Hypertension — previously mild at baseline; treatment-related worsening in the setting of bevacizumab and ramucirumab exposure; now on triple antihypertensive therapy
4. Oxaliplatin-induced peripheral sensory neuropathy — grade 3 at its worst (after cycle 10 of first-line), now improving to grade 1 following oxaliplatin discontinuation approximately 7 months ago
5. Former tobacco use — quit approximately 12 years prior to diagnosis; approximately 20 pack-year history

SURGICAL HISTORY

No prior abdominal surgery. No prior hepatic or pulmonary procedures. CT-guided lung biopsy (right lower lobe, at month 10 of illness) — uncomplicated.

FAMILY HISTORY

Father: colorectal cancer (age and stage at diagnosis not specified, deceased). No known hereditary colorectal cancer syndrome in the family. Germline Lynch syndrome and APC testing on the patient's NGS panel was negative.

SOCIAL HISTORY

Mr. Delgado is a high school teacher (currently on reduced duties). He is married with two adult children who are actively involved in his care and attend major appointments. He lives in a single-family home with his wife. He is a former smoker (quit 12 years ago, approximately 20 pack-year history). Denies current alcohol use. Denies illicit drug use. He maintains an active interest in staying as engaged with family life and work as his energy permits.

ALLERGIES

No known drug allergies. No prior infusion reactions to oxaliplatin, irinotecan, bevacizumab, or ramucirumab.

MEDICATIONS

1. Metformin 1000 mg orally twice daily (type 2 diabetes)
2. Amlodipine 10 mg orally once daily (hypertension)
3. Lisinopril 20 mg orally once daily (hypertension)
4. Hydrochlorothiazide 12.5 mg orally once daily (hypertension)
5. Gabapentin 300 mg orally at bedtime (residual oxaliplatin neuropathy — recently tapered from TID dosing)
6. Ondansetron 8 mg orally every 8 hours PRN nausea (active prescription from prior regimen)
7. Loperamide 2 mg PRN diarrhea
8. Acetaminophen 650 mg orally every 6 hours PRN abdominal pain
9. Multivitamin daily

---

PHYSICAL EXAMINATION

General: Mr. Delgado is a well-appearing, mildly fatigued Hispanic male in no acute distress. He appears his stated age. He is alert, oriented, and appropriately interactive, though there is a notable subdued quality to his affect today in the context of discussing his imaging results.

Vital Signs: Blood pressure 148/92 mmHg (mildly elevated today, likely white-coat effect in context of difficult news; will recheck), heart rate 82 beats per minute and regular, respiratory rate 16 breaths per minute, temperature 98.4°F, oxygen saturation 97% on room air, weight 174 lbs (down approximately 4 lbs from last visit 8 weeks ago). Height 5'9".

ECOG Performance Status: 2. The patient reports that fatigue now limits his activity — he is up and about for more than 50% of waking hours but has decreased capacity to perform normal daily tasks, can no longer work full-time, and has reduced physical exertion compared to 2 months ago. He remains ambulatory and capable of self-care.

HEENT: Normocephalic, atraumatic. Mucous membranes moist. No oral mucositis or thrush. Sclerae anicteric. No conjunctival pallor.

Neck: Supple. No cervical or supraclavicular lymphadenopathy palpable.

Cardiovascular: Regular rate and rhythm. No murmurs, rubs, or gallops. No peripheral edema in the bilateral lower extremities.

Pulmonary: Clear to auscultation bilaterally. No wheezes, rales, or rhonchi. No dullness to percussion. Respiratory effort unlabored.

Abdomen: Soft. Mildly tender to deep palpation in the right upper quadrant and epigastrium, without guarding or rebound. Liver edge palpable approximately 2 cm below the right costal margin, slightly firm — consistent with hepatic metastatic burden. Spleen not enlarged. Bowel sounds active in all four quadrants. No visible distension. No ascites on clinical exam today (no flank dullness or shifting dullness detected).

Musculoskeletal: Normal gait. No focal bony tenderness on axial or appendicular palpation.

Neurological: Alert and oriented x3. Cranial nerves II-XII grossly intact. Strength 5/5 in bilateral upper and lower extremities. Sensation mildly diminished to light touch in bilateral feet below the ankle — consistent with residual oxaliplatin neuropathy (grade 1). No upper extremity sensory deficit noted today. Reflexes 2+ throughout.

Skin: No rash, petechiae, or purpura. No jaundice or icterus. No hand-foot syndrome or palmar-plantar changes.

Lymph nodes: No axillary, inguinal, or palpable abdominal lymphadenopathy.

---

LABORATORY RESULTS

Complete Blood Count:
- WBC 5.8 x10^9/L (normal differential)
- Hemoglobin 11.1 g/dL (mild anemia, normocytic — down from 12.3 g/dL at last check, likely disease-related/treatment-related)
- Platelets 218 x10^9/L
- ANC 3.1 x10^9/L

Comprehensive Metabolic Panel:
- Sodium 138 mEq/L, Potassium 4.0 mEq/L, Creatinine 0.98 mg/dL (eGFR >60 mL/min/1.73m²)
- ALT 52 U/L (mildly elevated, up from 38 at last visit)
- AST 61 U/L (mildly elevated)
- Alkaline phosphatase 148 U/L (elevated, up from 112 — likely reflecting increasing hepatic tumor burden)
- Total bilirubin 0.9 mg/dL (normal)
- Albumin 3.5 g/dL (low-normal)
- Total protein 6.8 g/dL

Tumor Markers:
- CEA: 287 ng/mL (up from 112 ng/mL at last assessment 8 weeks prior — significant rise, consistent with disease progression)
- CA 19-9: 201 U/mL (up from 94 U/mL — also rising)

Other:
- HbA1c: 7.1% (diabetes reasonably controlled)
- PT/INR: 1.1 (normal)
- Magnesium: 1.8 mg/dL (low-normal)
- Urinalysis: no proteinuria (reassuring in context of ongoing anti-VEGF exposure)

---

IMAGING RESULTS

Most recent CT scan of the chest, abdomen, and pelvis with contrast (performed approximately 1 week prior to today's visit — reviewed in detail today):

Compared with CT from approximately 3 months ago (after 4 cycles of FOLFIRI/ramucirumab), this scan demonstrates clear disease progression across multiple sites.

Pulmonary: Three new pulmonary nodules identified compared to prior: left lower lobe 0.8 cm, right middle lobe 0.6 cm, and lingula of the left upper lobe 0.5 cm. The pre-existing right lower lobe nodule is stable at 1.1 cm and the right upper lobe nodule stable at 0.7 cm. Total bilateral pulmonary nodule burden has increased and now comprises at least 5 discrete nodules. The appearance remains consistent with metastatic disease.

Liver: The segment VI lesion has grown from 2.3 cm to 2.9 cm. A new 0.8 cm lesion is identified in segment V, not present on prior imaging. The segment IVa, segment II, and segment VII lesions remain stable at 1.1 cm, 0.7 cm, and 0.4 cm respectively. Overall hepatic tumor burden has increased.

Colon/Primary Tumor: The sigmoid colon primary mass measures 3.4 cm (slight increase from 3.1 cm on prior scan). No evidence of obstruction. Pericolonic fat stranding is unchanged.

Peritoneum/Ascites: No definitive ascites or peritoneal implants identified on this scan.

Bones/Other: No lytic or blastic bone lesions identified. No pathological lymphadenopathy beyond what was previously documented. No brain imaging was performed on this scan.

Overall imaging impression: Progressive disease on second-line therapy with new pulmonary metastatic deposits and growth of the dominant hepatic lesion with a new hepatic lesion in segment V. The disease burden has increased in both the lungs and liver compared with the prior assessment.

---

ASSESSMENT AND PLAN

Mr. Marcus Delgado is a 59-year-old man with Stage IV sigmoid colon adenocarcinoma (KRAS G12D, MSS, TMB-low, left-sided primary) who presents today with unequivocal radiographic and biochemical disease progression after approximately 7 months on second-line FOLFIRI plus ramucirumab. His CEA has more than doubled in 8 weeks and his imaging now shows new pulmonary nodules in both lungs as well as interval growth of the dominant liver metastasis and a new hepatic segment V deposit. This is his second progression event and his disease has now spread to involve the liver, lungs, and sigmoid colon primary (still in situ, not obstructing).

His performance status has declined to ECOG PS 2 — he is managing self-care but his activity level is meaningfully reduced from his baseline. Mild anemia (Hgb 11.1), mildly elevated transaminases and alkaline phosphatase (suggesting modest hepatic parenchymal compromise from increasing metastatic burden), and weight loss of approximately 4 lbs are also noted.

Key oncologic considerations:

1. DISEASE PROGRESSION THROUGH TWO LINES OF THERAPY: He has now progressed through FOLFOX/bevacizumab (first-line) and FOLFIRI/ramucirumab (second-line). KRAS G12D continues to preclude any use of EGFR-directed antibody therapy (cetuximab, panitumumab) regardless of line. His MSS/TMB-low status precludes checkpoint immunotherapy. HER2 is not amplified (IHC 1+, copy number 2.1 on NGS), so HER2-directed therapy is not applicable.

2. THIRD-LINE OPTIONS REVIEWED IN DETAIL:

   a. Regorafenib (Stivarga): A multikinase inhibitor with demonstrated OS benefit in chemotherapy-refractory mCRC (CORRECT trial — median OS 6.4 vs 5.0 months). However, regorafenib carries a significant toxicity burden including hand-foot skin reaction, fatigue, hypertension, and hepatotoxicity. Given Mr. Delgado already has mildly elevated liver function tests and poorly controlled hypertension at baseline, I have concerns about tolerability.

   b. TAS-102 (trifluridine/tipiracil, Lonsurf) monotherapy: An oral nucleoside analog with demonstrated OS benefit in refractory mCRC (RECOURSE trial). Manageable toxicity profile with primarily myelosuppression. Can be used as a single agent.

   c. TAS-102 plus bevacizumab (SUNLIGHT regimen): Based on the SUNLIGHT trial (published in NEJM 2023), the combination of TAS-102 and bevacizumab demonstrated significantly improved OS compared to TAS-102 monotherapy in refractory mCRC (median OS 10.8 vs 7.5 months; HR 0.61). This combination has emerged as a preferred approach in patients who are fit for this therapy. Mr. Delgado has previously received bevacizumab in first-line without significant toxicity (the dose-limiting toxicity was hypertension driven primarily by the VEGFR-2 mechanism with ramucirumab; bevacizumab-related hypertension was grade 1 in first-line and was managed with single-agent amlodipine).

   d. Clinical trial enrollment: Discussed the landscape of KRAS G12D-directed clinical trials, including investigational KRAS G12D inhibitors (e.g., MRTX1133 in combination studies) and other investigational approaches. Mr. Delgado was counseled on available trials and the potential benefit of enrolling. He has previously declined trial participation, and after today's discussion, he expressed willingness to consider trials but requested to first try an approved regimen. We will submit a referral to our clinical trials office for screening, particularly for any available KRAS G12D-directed studies.

3. PLAN — THIRD-LINE THERAPY: After thorough discussion with Mr. Delgado and his wife, weighing the efficacy data, his current performance status (ECOG 2), his prior treatment exposures, and his goals of care, we will initiate third-line TAS-102 (trifluridine/tipiracil, Lonsurf) 35 mg/m2 orally twice daily with food on days 1-5 and days 8-12 of each 28-day cycle, in combination with bevacizumab 5 mg/kg IV every 2 weeks (days 1 and 15), per the SUNLIGHT trial regimen. I reviewed the oral medication dosing schedule carefully with him and his wife to ensure understanding — he will take the tablets twice daily (morning and evening) with meals on the designated days, and return every 2 weeks for bevacizumab infusions and CBC checks.

   Dose calculation: BSA approximately 1.95 m². TAS-102 dose: 35 mg/m² = ~68 mg per dose, rounded to 70 mg per dose (using available 15 mg and 20 mg tablet combinations). Bevacizumab: 5 mg/kg = ~395 mg (rounded per pharmacy).

   Monitoring: CBC with differential every 2 weeks, given myelosuppression as the primary TAS-102 toxicity. CMP and LFTs every cycle. Blood pressure monitoring at each infusion. Urinalysis for proteinuria each cycle (bevacizumab requirement). Tumor markers (CEA, CA 19-9) and CT restaging after 3 cycles (approximately 3 months).

4. PERFORMANCE STATUS AND SUPPORTIVE CARE: His ECOG PS has declined to 2. This is an important consideration as further PS decline may limit tolerability of even this relatively less toxic regimen. Reinforcing nutritional support — he has mild hypoalbuminemia and unintentional weight loss. Referred to clinical dietitian (appointment to be scheduled). Continued optimization of anemia is warranted: iron studies and B12/folate levels ordered today. If iron-deficient anemia identified, will treat with iron supplementation. ESA use is not indicated in this actively treated metastatic setting.

5. PAIN AND SYMPTOM MANAGEMENT: Right upper quadrant discomfort is currently mild and managed adequately with acetaminophen. If pain worsens, will reassess and escalate analgesic therapy (low-dose opioid may be appropriate if acetaminophen insufficient). Nausea prophylaxis: ondansetron PRN. Fatigue: I advised reasonable pacing of activity, brief naps if needed, and avoidance of isolation — maintaining social engagement with family has been shown to support quality of life. No formal prescription for a CNS stimulant at this time given his performance status and the fact that he is starting a new regimen.

6. PALLIATIVE CARE: Mr. Delgado has been engaged with outpatient palliative care since approximately month 14 of his illness, and I strongly encourage continued close co-management. As his disease has now progressed through two lines of therapy with a declining performance status, the palliative care team's involvement in goals-of-care conversations, advance care planning, and symptom management will become increasingly important. I have communicated today's visit summary to the palliative care team.

7. GOALS OF CARE: We had a frank and compassionate discussion today about the trajectory of his illness. He understands that his disease has progressed through two systemic therapy regimens and that each line of therapy, statistically, tends to yield smaller responses and shorter durations of disease control. He was told clearly that TAS-102/bevacizumab represents a reasonable option with data supporting a median survival of approximately 10-11 months in the trial population, but that individual outcomes vary widely. He expressed that he values being able to remain active and present for his wife and children, to continue some teaching if possible, and to avoid hospitalizations. He does not wish to discuss hospice at this time but has acknowledged that this would be an appropriate future discussion as circumstances evolve. His values and wishes were documented.

8. HYPERTENSION: Blood pressure today 148/92 mmHg — mildly elevated on current triple regimen. He had an episode of grade 3 hypertension on ramucirumab (VEGFR-2-directed mechanism); bevacizumab in first-line was associated with grade 1 HTN. I anticipate bevacizumab will be better tolerated from a blood pressure standpoint. Will obtain repeat BP reading at end of visit. If persistently above 145/90 mmHg, will contact cardiology and consider further antihypertensive adjustment.

9. DIABETES MANAGEMENT: Metformin continued. HbA1c 7.1% — acceptable. Will monitor given that corticosteroid use as pre-medication (dexamethasone) may cause transient hyperglycemia; patient and care team should be aware.

FOLLOW-UP: Mr. Delgado will return in 2 weeks for cycle 1 day 15 bevacizumab infusion and CBC. Next full clinic visit with oncology review will be in approximately 4 weeks (after cycle 2 day 1). Pharmacy will process the TAS-102 (Lonsurf) prescription for oral dispensing today. Patient and wife received written instructions on oral medication adherence, when to call for toxicity (fever, diarrhea, mouth sores, unusual bleeding or bruising), and emergency contact numbers.

Patient and wife expressed understanding of the plan and elected to proceed. Questions addressed.

Electronically signed,
Gastrointestinal Medical Oncology Attending
